section.title: Do Not Be Unequally Yoked
section.sectionRationale: Into the appeal for open hearts Paul sets a sharp call to separation from the defiling entanglements of an idolatrous world. Through five rhetorical contrasts he shows there can be no true partnership between righteousness and wickedness, and he grounds the call in the staggering privilege that believers are the temple of the living God, in whom God has promised to dwell. On that basis he issues the summons to come out and be separate, and the therefore of 7:1: cleanse ourselves and perfect holiness in the fear of God.
section.thesis: Because believers are the temple of the living God in whom He dwells, they must not bind themselves in compromising partnership with unbelief; rather, holding God's own promises, they are to come out, be separate, and cleanse themselves, perfecting holiness in the fear of God.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the command not to be unequally yoked, unfolded in five contrasts; the identity of believers as the temple of the living God with its cluster of promises; and the summons to come out and be separate, issuing in the call to perfect holiness in the fear of God.

units.label: Five contrasts that cannot be joined
units.note: no partnership between light and darkness

verses.text: Do not be {{heterozygeo|unequally yoked}} with unbelievers. For what partnership can righteousness have with wickedness? Or what fellowship does light have with darkness?
What harmony is there between Christ and Belial? Or what does a believer have in common with an unbeliever?

units.label: You are the temple of the living God
units.note: I will dwell with them and be their God

verses.text: What agreement can exist between the {{naos|temple}} of God and idols? For we are the temple of the living God. As God has said: “I will dwell with them and walk among them, and I will be their God, and they will be My people.”
“Therefore come out from among them and be separate, says the Lord. Touch no unclean thing, and I will receive you.”
And: “I will be a Father to you, and you will be My sons and daughters, says the Lord Almighty.”

units.label: Therefore perfect holiness
units.note: cleanse ourselves in the fear of God

verses.text: Therefore, beloved, since we have these promises, let us cleanse ourselves from everything that defiles body and spirit, perfecting {{hagiosyne|holiness}} in the fear of God.

heterozygeo.gloss: To be yoked together with something of a different kind, to be mismatched in a common harness. The image recalls the law forbidding an ox and a donkey to be yoked together to plow. Paul applies it to believers binding themselves in intimate, controlling partnership with unbelievers, where the two are pulling toward opposite masters. It warns against entanglements that compromise loyalty to Christ, not against all contact with unbelievers.

naos.gloss: The temple, and specifically the inner sanctuary where God's presence dwelt, the holy place rather than the temple courts. Paul says the community of believers is the naos of the living God, the very dwelling place of His presence. This is why compromise with idols is unthinkable: the God who once filled the tabernacle and temple now dwells among and within His people. The dignity of the temple grounds the call to holiness.

hagiosyne.gloss: Holiness, the state of being set apart wholly for God. Perfecting hagiosyne is not earning acceptance but bringing to completion the consecration that belongs to those God has claimed as His temple and His sons and daughters. It is pursued in the fear of God, a reverent awe of the holy One who dwells with His people. Holiness here is both gift and calling, rooted in the promises just cited.

basic.context: Right after asking the Corinthians to open their hearts, Paul warns them not to bind themselves in compromising partnership with unbelievers. He drives it home with five sharp contrasts: righteousness and wickedness, light and darkness, Christ and Belial, believer and unbeliever, the temple of God and idols. Then he lifts their eyes to who they are: the temple of the living God, in whom God promised to dwell. On that basis he calls them to come out, be separate, and cleanse themselves, bringing holiness to completion in the fear of God.
basic.hermeneutics: This is not a call to leave the world or to shun all unbelievers, which Paul forbids elsewhere. It is a call to refuse any binding alliance where loyalty to Christ would be compromised and idolatry shared. The weight of the argument falls on identity: because you are God's temple, you cannot house idols. The chain of Old Testament promises fuels a therefore, not a fear; holiness flows from being loved and indwelt by God.
basic.originalLanguages: The command uses heterozygeo, to be yoked with a different kind, recalling the law against plowing with mismatched animals. The reason is that believers are the naos, the inner sanctuary, the dwelling place of God's presence. And the goal is hagiosyne, holiness, brought to completion in reverent fear of the God who dwells with His people.
basic.typology: The temple where God dwelt among Israel is fulfilled in the church, the living temple indwelt by His Spirit, and ultimately in Christ, the true temple. The command come out and be separate echoes the exodus from Egypt and the call out of Babylon; God's people are always a redeemed people called out to belong to Him alone.
basic.christ: Christ is set in absolute opposition to Belial: there is no harmony between them, and to belong to Christ is to be done with the works of darkness. Christ is the true temple and the one in whom God dwells with His people, and by His Spirit believers become that temple. The promises I will be their God and you will be My sons and daughters are secured in Him.
basic.god: God is the living God who dwells among His people, not a dead idol. He is the covenant Lord who promises I will be their God and they will be My people, and the Father who receives sons and daughters. He is the Lord Almighty whose holiness both welcomes and demands: He receives the separate and calls the received to perfect holiness in His fear.
basic.crossReferences: Leviticus 26 and Ezekiel 37 promise God dwelling among His people. Isaiah 52 is the call to come out and touch no unclean thing. First Corinthians 6 also calls the believer's body the temple of the Holy Spirit.
basic.oneStory: From Eden to the tabernacle to the temple, God has sought to dwell among His people, and sin has always threatened that dwelling. In Christ, God makes His people the temple where He lives by His Spirit, and He calls them out from idolatry to belong wholly to Him, until holiness is made complete in His presence.

context.literary: Verses 14 to 7:1 interrupt the personal appeal that runs from 6:11 to 13 and resumes at 7:2, which has led many to discuss its place in the letter. Whatever its origin, in the received text it functions as the sharp edge of the appeal to open their hearts to Paul: opening to him means closing to idolatry. The unit is built as a chain of five rhetorical questions, a catena of Old Testament quotations, and a concluding therefore in 7:1.
context.historical: Corinth was saturated with pagan temples, idol feasts, and cults, and believers faced constant pressure to keep old religious and social ties. To be unequally yoked would include shared participation in idolatrous worship and any binding partnership that entangled them with the pagan cult. Paul's earlier letter had already wrestled with food offered to idols and temple banquets, so the danger was concrete and pressing.
context.canonical: The promises quoted are woven from Leviticus 26:12, Ezekiel 37:27, Isaiah 52:11, and 2 Samuel 7:14, gathering the covenant, the temple, the new exodus, and the Davidic sonship into one appeal. The temple of the living God theme runs from the tabernacle through the prophets to the New Testament church and the new Jerusalem, where God dwells with His people forever.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To call the Corinthians to decisive separation from idolatrous and compromising alliances, grounding the summons in their identity as God's indwelt temple and in His covenant promises, so that they pursue holiness out of gratitude and reverence rather than fear or legalism.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The command not to be unequally yoked and to perfect holiness is prescriptive for the church in every age. What is descriptive is the particular Corinthian setting of pagan temples and idol feasts; readers must discern the analogous entanglements of their own day rather than reduce the text to a single application.

debates.question: Is 6:14 to 7:1 an original part of the letter or an inserted digression?

views.label: An original Pauline digression
views.case: The passage fits Paul's argument as the demand behind the appeal to open their hearts, and the manuscripts uniformly include it here; Paul often breaks off and returns, as he does at 7:2.

views.label: A later insertion or reused fragment
views.case: Some scholars note the abrupt break between 6:13 and 7:2, distinctive vocabulary, and the dense catena of quotations, suggesting a separate piece placed here in editing.

debates.bearing: Either way the passage is canonical Scripture and functions as the sharp edge of the surrounding appeal; the call to separation and holiness stands with full authority in its present place.

debates.question: What is the scope of do not be unequally yoked?

views.label: All binding, compromising partnerships with unbelief
views.case: The five contrasts are broad, so the primary sense is any intimate alliance, business, religious, or social, in which loyalty to Christ is compromised and idolatry shared.

views.label: Chiefly marriage to an unbeliever
views.case: A long tradition applies the yoke especially to marriage, since it is the most binding of partnerships, and reads it as counsel against believers marrying unbelievers.

debates.bearing: The broad reading is primary and marriage is a clear application within it; the point is not withdrawal from all contact with unbelievers, which Paul forbids, but refusing entanglements that divide the heart from Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The governing image is heterozygeo, to be yoked with a creature of another kind, recalling the law that forbade plowing with an ox and a donkey together; two mismatched animals cannot pull as one, and neither can a believer bound to unbelief. Paul asks five times what these opposites could share, and answers with the church's identity: we are the naos, not the outer courts but the inner sanctuary, the very dwelling place of the living God's presence. Because idols cannot cohabit with that presence, God says come out and be separate. The concluding aim is hagiosyne, holiness, the completed consecration of a people wholly set apart, pursued in the reverent fear of the God who has made them His temple, His people, and His sons and daughters.

typology.type: The tabernacle and temple where God dwelt
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the church as the temple of the living God, indwelt by His Spirit, and in Christ the true temple in whom God dwells with His people.

typology.type: Come out and be separate, the new exodus
typology.fulfillment: The call out of Egypt and Babylon is fulfilled as God calls His people out of idolatry into exclusive covenant belonging, consummated in the holy city where He dwells with them forever.

study.christ: Christ stands in total opposition to Belial; light and darkness cannot mingle, and to belong to Christ is to have finished with idols. He is the true temple, and by union with Him and the indwelling Spirit believers become the temple of the living God. The whole cluster of promises, I will be their God, you will be My sons and daughters, is secured in Christ, in whom all God's promises are Yes and Amen. His holiness is both the pattern and the power for the holiness His people are called to perfect.
study.god: God is the living God, sharply distinguished from lifeless idols, who chooses to dwell among and within His people. He is the covenant Lord who says I will be their God and they will be My people, and the Father Almighty who welcomes sons and daughters. His holiness both attracts and demands: He receives those who come out and are separate, and He calls the received to cleanse themselves and perfect holiness in reverent fear of Him. His promises are the ground of the entire summons.

crossReferences.note: God's promise to make His dwelling among His people and walk among them, quoted in verse 16.
Come out, be separate, touch no unclean thing, the source of the call to separation in verse 17.
The believer's body is the temple of the Holy Spirit, so glorify God in it, paralleling the temple identity here.
The promise I will be a Father to him and he will be a son to Me, broadened here to all God's sons and daughters.

study.oneStory: From Eden onward God has sought to dwell with His people, and idolatry has always threatened that fellowship. In Christ, God makes His people the very temple where He lives by His Spirit, and He calls them out of every idolatrous entanglement to belong to Him alone. Holding His promises, they pursue a holiness that will be made complete when He dwells with them forever in the new creation.

quiz.q: What does Paul mean by do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers?

options.text: Do not bind yourself in compromising partnership where loyalty to Christ is divided.
options.note: The yoke image warns against entanglements that share in idolatry and split the heart from Christ.

options.text: Never have any contact or friendship with unbelievers at all.
options.note: Paul elsewhere forbids total withdrawal; the issue is binding, compromising alliances.

options.text: Only that believers should worship in their own buildings.
options.note: The point is loyalty and holiness, not merely a place of worship.

options.text: That unbelievers are worthless and beneath believers.
options.note: Paul longs for unbelievers to be reconciled to God; the concern is compromise, not contempt.

quiz.q: On what basis does Paul ground his call to separation?

options.text: That believers are the temple of the living God in whom He dwells.
options.note: The identity as God's temple makes fellowship with idols unthinkable.

options.text: That unbelievers will always ruin a believer's reputation.
options.note: The ground is God's indwelling presence, not concern for reputation.

options.text: That the law of Moses forbids all mixing of peoples.
options.note: Paul cites the temple and the promises, not ethnic separation.

options.text: That separation earns a believer's salvation.
options.note: Holiness flows from being God's temple, it does not earn God's favor.

quiz.q: What promises does Paul cite to move them toward holiness?

options.text: I will dwell with them, be their God, and be a Father to them, and they will be My sons and daughters.
options.note: A chain of covenant promises of God's dwelling and adoption grounds the therefore of 7:1.

options.text: That they will become wealthy if they obey.
options.note: The promises are of God's presence and adoption, not material reward.

options.text: That they will never suffer again.
options.note: Paul has just described his own suffering; the promises are of God's dwelling, not ease.

options.text: That the law will finally be abolished.
options.note: The promises concern God's covenant presence, not the abolition of the law.

quiz.q: What is the therefore of 2 Corinthians 7:1?

options.text: Since we have these promises, cleanse ourselves and perfect holiness in the fear of God.
options.note: The promises fuel a call to complete holiness in reverent fear of God.

options.text: Since we have these promises, we may now live as we please.
options.note: The promises lead to holiness, not to license.

options.text: Since we have these promises, we should judge all unbelievers.
options.note: The call is to cleanse ourselves, not to condemn others.

options.text: Since we have these promises, holiness no longer matters.
options.note: The whole point is to perfect holiness in the fear of God.

questions: Paul warns against being unequally yoked. What binding partnerships or alliances in your life pull your loyalty away from Christ?
Believers are the temple of the living God, His dwelling place. How would truly believing that God lives in you reshape what you allow into your life?
The call to holiness rests on the promises I will be their God and you will be My sons and daughters. How does being loved and adopted by God change the way you pursue holiness?
Come out and be separate is a call to decisive action. Is there something God is asking you to walk away from in order to belong to Him alone?
Paul says to cleanse ourselves from everything that defiles body and spirit. What specific defilement is God prompting you to leave behind in the fear of Him?

translationNotes.note: Unequally yoked renders heterozygeo, a mismatched yoke, echoing the law against plowing with an ox and donkey together; the sense is a binding partnership that divides loyalty, not all association with unbelievers.
Belial is a name for the evil one; the BSB footnote notes many Greek sources read Beliar, a variant spelling. Either way it names the utter opposite of Christ.
The quotations weave together Leviticus 26:12, Ezekiel 37:27, Isaiah 52:11, and 2 Samuel 7:14, set as poetry in the source and rendered here as prose within the verses, keeping the quotation marks.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 Corinthians 6:14-18 and 7:1. No em dashes required substitution in this passage. The Old Testament quotations in 6:16-18 are set as poetry in the source and rendered here as prose inside the verses, keeping the quotation marks. Sermon set to none; not verified this session.
